methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $20M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $250M - $400.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Palantir
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $400B+

private.name: Liquid AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2.35B

private.name: Field AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Twelve Labs
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $250M

data.narrative:
Gladia is a Series A–stage AI & Software company that has raised $20M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer set of 4 same-sector, same-stage comparables spanning both private and public markets, anchored by Palantir (public), Liquid AI, Field AI, and Twelve Labs.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The Stage-Progression method uses the $20M total raised as a baseline and applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement, implying a $40M valuation. The Peer-Median method calculates the median valuation across the 4 comparables at $2.2B, with a range spanning $250M (Twelve Labs floor) to $400B (Palantir ceiling). The wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in the AI & Software sector at this stage.

Triangulating both methods yields a range of $34M to $2.5B, with a median of $1.1B. The Stage-Progression approach anchors the low end, while Peer-Median dominates the central and high scenarios. Key uncertainties include the outsized influence of Palantir's $400B valuation on the peer median—which may not reflect Gladia's near-term trajectory—and structural differences in product maturity, revenue stage, and market positioning across the 4-company sample. A valuation toward the lower quartile of the peer range may be more defensible absent additional revenue or traction data.
